definition: Apple production in metric tons by province in 1974, from ODEPA's Estadísticas Agropecuarias fruit returns.

breaks.label: CONARA regionalization (13 regions replace provinces)
breaks.description: Decreto-Ley 575 of July 1974, implemented through 1975-1976, replaced Chile's 25-province structure with 13 regions plus the Metropolitan Region. Department-level reporting effectively ends; from this date forward the canonical subnational geography is the region (XV regions today, after later subdivisions).
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 575 de 1974; ODEPLAN/CONARA (1976), Chile hacia un nuevo destino.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Department and province series should not be drawn past 1974 without a notice; the data after this date originates from region-level reporting back-cast to the older units, which is interpolation. Recommended: terminate department/province series at 1973 by default with an option to view the back-cast extension.

label: Barley production
definition: Annual national production of barley in metric tons, compiled from the Anuario Estadístico and Sinopsis series through 1929 and from the Direccion General de Estadistica (and INE after 1970) thereafter. Pre-1880 figures are partial and reflect the agricultural reporting capacity of the early Anuarios.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: Bean production
definition: Annual national production of dry beans in metric tons. Beans were a staple of the Chilean diet and a significant 19th-century export to neighboring Andean economies; the series reflects both subsistence and export production.

label: Cattle output
definition: Annual head of cattle slaughtered nationally for meat. The series ends in 1940 because later cattle statistics changed methodology and unit (carcass weight rather than head); the post-1940 figures live under a different indicator family.

breaks.label: Provincial reorganization (DFL 8,582)
breaks.description: Decreto con Fuerza de Ley 8,582 of 1927, in force 1928, redrew Chile's provincial map. Some 19th-century departments merged, new provinces were created from departmental groupings, and several department names persisted into different boundary contexts. The 52-panel that anchors this dataset is built from the pre-1928 frame; post-1928 departments map imperfectly to it.
breaks.citation: Decreto con Fuerza de Ley 8,582 de 1927; Cavieres (1988), División político-administrativa de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: All department and province series should annotate 1928 as a boundary discontinuity. Cross-break comparisons of the same named department before and after 1928 are not on the same geography and should carry a notice. The platform's choice of 1920 as the last department GeoJSON frame predates this reform; users mapping post-1928 data onto pre-1928 geometry are looking at a fiction.

label: Population (extended panel)
curated_subtitle: Province population, extended panel 1843–1935 (to be retired)
definition: Province-level total population from a Mamalakis (1980) Vol 2 compilation extending the census panel with intercensal interpolations. Per the concept-duplication resolution (deliverable 01, Section 1.1), this indicator is scheduled for retirement: its cells fold into the canonical total_population at province scale, with the interpolation years carrying explicit attribution.

breaks.label: Provincial reorganization (DFL 8,582)
breaks.description: Decreto con Fuerza de Ley 8,582 of 1927, in force 1928, redrew Chile's provincial map. Some 19th-century departments merged, new provinces were created from departmental groupings, and several department names persisted into different boundary contexts. The 52-panel that anchors this dataset is built from the pre-1928 frame; post-1928 departments map imperfectly to it.
breaks.citation: Decreto con Fuerza de Ley 8,582 de 1927; Cavieres (1988), División político-administrativa de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: All department and province series should annotate 1928 as a boundary discontinuity. Cross-break comparisons of the same named department before and after 1928 are not on the same geography and should carry a notice. The platform's choice of 1920 as the last department GeoJSON frame predates this reform; users mapping post-1928 data onto pre-1928 geometry are looking at a fiction.

breaks.label: First modern census
breaks.description: The 1865 census was Chile's first under the Oficina Central de Estadística using modern enumeration. Pre-1865 'censuses' (Egaña 1813, 1835, 1843, 1854) were proto-statistical exercises with incomplete territorial coverage, varying methodologies, and known undercounts. The manifest_globals.json policy carries '[pre_1865_census=...;caveats=...]' notes on affected cells; the platform must surface these caveats prominently.
breaks.citation: manifest_globals.json; methodology decisions M024, M025, M037.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Pre-1865 population dots should render with an open marker (not filled) and a caption noting 'proto-statistical, undercount likely.' This is not a break but a continuous caveat on a year range.

label: Population density
curated_subtitle: Population density at province scale, 1900–1930, persons per km²
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. ca. 1858. Sinopsis Estadística i Jeográfica de la República de Chile. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1908. Censo de la República de Chile levantado el 28 de Noviembre de 1907. Santiago: Sociedad Imprenta y Litografía Universo.
Dirección General de Estadística. 1931. X Censo de Población de la República de Chile, 27 de noviembre de 1930. Santiago.
source_document: Sinopsis Estadística i Jeográfica de Chile (19th c.)
definition: Province-level population density, persons per square kilometer, computed as the ratio of total province population to the province's territorial extent. Coverage is sparse (three observation years) and the territorial denominator predates the 1928 administrative reorganization for two of the three years.

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: Agricultural GDP
definition: Real GDP of the agricultural sector (crops, livestock, forestry, fishing) in constant 1996 pesos. The agricultural share of Chilean GDP fell from roughly 30 percent in 1860 to under 10 percent by 1990; this series shows the absolute level behind that compositional shift.

label: Agricultural exports
definition: Value of agricultural exports (crops, livestock products, wood, fish). Chile's agricultural exports peaked in absolute and relative terms during the 1850s–1880s wheat-export cycle and then declined relative to mining exports.

label: Capital goods imports
definition: Value of capital-goods imports (machinery, transportation equipment, construction materials). The series ends in 1937; later capital-goods imports are tracked under different indicators that follow the 1929 customs-classification revision.
units_note: millions of US dollars

label: Construction GDP
definition: Real GDP of the construction sector in constant 1996 pesos. The series tracks the major Chilean public works booms (the 1880s nitrate-revenue construction, the 1930s import-substitution infrastructure, the 1960s Frei-era housing, the 1990s post-transition expansion).

label: Defense share of expenditure (provisional — outliers)
curated_subtitle: Defense as share of fiscal expenditure, 1833–1990, percent
definition: Military and defense outlays as a percentage of total central-government fiscal expenditure. The published series carries unit-splice corruption: maximum observed value is 99,952 against a natural ceiling of 100, indicating raw expenditure values were spliced into the share series across at least part of the period. Do not cite this series until the upstream reconciliation lands; the underlying primary sources (Memoria de Hacienda, Mamalakis 1980) remain usable.

label: Exports (% of GDP)
curated_subtitle: Exports as share of GDP, 1810–1990, percent
definition: Total exports as a percentage of GDP. The series likely carries the same unit-splice family as the other ratio series in this block; verify before citing.

breaks.label: Tarapacá annexed (Treaty of Ancón)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Ancón (October 20, 1883) transferred Tarapacá Province from Peru to Chile. National-level population, fiscal-revenue, and saltpeter series jump because the territorial base grew; department- and province-level series are unaffected outside Tarapacá itself.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Ancón, October 20, 1883; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1883 on national-aggregate series. For department/province maps, Tarapacá appears in the data starting 1884 (1883 was a transition year); the choropleth should show this as new geography rather than missing data.

breaks.label: Antofagasta annexed (Pacto de Tregua)
breaks.description: The Pacto de Tregua of April 4, 1884 transferred Antofagasta from Bolivia to Chilean administration; the formal cession came with the Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904. The 1884 pact is the working date for the data; Antofagasta enters Chilean statistical reporting from this year.
breaks.citation: Pacto de Tregua, 4 de abril de 1884; Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1884. The pre-1883 baseline excludes both Tarapacá and Antofagasta and is the relevant comparison year for the new territories.

breaks.label: Dirección General de Estadística replaces OCE
breaks.description: The Oficina Central de Estadística (1843–1927) was reorganized into the Dirección General de Estadística in 1927. Several series carry definitional revisions across this transition (occupational classifications, education tabulations, foreign-trade reporting).
breaks.citation: Decreto sobre la Dirección General de Estadística, 1927; Hutchison (2001), Labors Appropriate to Their Sex.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption should attribute pre-1929 cells to OCE and post-1929 cells to DGE; no vertical rule required unless the user opens a definitional caveat.

breaks.label: Anuario primary → Braun et al. compilation
breaks.description: For long-run national series (GDP, fiscal aggregates, foreign trade, demographics), the pre-1929 segment is typically primary archival readings from the Anuario, Sinopsis, and Memoria series, while the post-1929 segment relies on Braun et al. (1998), Mamalakis (1980), and Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner (DLW) compilations. The compilers harmonized definitions, sometimes with judgment calls that differ from the primary readings.
breaks.citation: Braun et al. (1998); Mamalakis (1980); Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption attributes by year-segment per audit Finding 4.2. No vertical rule required unless a definitional revision crossed this date.

label: External debt (YoY % change)
curated_subtitle: External public debt as share of GDP, 1822–1990, percent
definition: Outstanding external public debt as a percentage of GDP. The Chilean sovereign debt history includes major restructurings (1830s default, 1842 settlement, 1879 War of the Pacific suspension, 1932 default, 1980s rescheduling) that produce step-changes in the series; these are real, not data artifacts.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.

label: External public debt
curated_subtitle: External public debt outstanding, 1822–1990, millions of pesos
definition: Outstanding stock of external public debt in millions of pesos. The nominal-peso unit makes this series uninterpretable across the 1925, 1960, and 1975 currency reforms without deflation; the break-annotation register flags these.

breaks.label: Central Bank founded; peso pegged to gold
breaks.description: The Kemmerer mission of 1925 created the Banco Central de Chile (Decree-Law 486, August 1925) and pegged the peso to gold at 6 pence sterling. Pre-1925 fiscal and monetary series are nominal-peso, post-1925 reflect the gold-standard regime through its 1932 abandonment.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 486 de 1925, creación del Banco Central de Chile; Edwin W. Kemmerer (1925), Report on the Central Bank of Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Draw a vertical rule at 1925; tooltip names Central Bank founding and the gold peg.

breaks.label: Escudo replaces peso (1,000 : 1)
breaks.description: By Decree-Law 13.305 of 1959 the escudo replaced the peso at 1 escudo = 1,000 old pesos, in force January 1, 1960. Any nominal series that crosses 1960 without a 1,000× rescaling will show a phantom 1,000-fold drop interpreted as economic collapse.
breaks.citation: Ley 13.305 de 1959; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Refuse to plot a nominal-currency series across 1960 without deflation. If the platform has no deflated companion series, gray the post-1960 segment and surface a notice. Real-peso, constant-1996-peso, and GBP series are unaffected and render normally.

breaks.label: New peso replaces escudo (1,000 : 1)
breaks.description: By Decree-Law 1,123 of September 29, 1975 the current peso replaced the escudo at 1 peso = 1,000 escudos, in force September 29, 1975. Any nominal series that crosses 1975 without rescaling will show a second phantom 1,000-fold drop on top of the 1960 break.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 1,123 de 1975; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Same treatment as the 1960 break. Cumulative effect of 1960 + 1975 means a 1959 escudo value and a 1975 peso value are 1,000,000× apart in nominal terms.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: External taxes share of revenue
curated_subtitle: Customs revenue as share of fiscal revenue, 1833–1990, percent
definition: Revenue from customs duties and other taxes on foreign trade as a percentage of total fiscal revenue. The published series carries unit-splice corruption (max 2,034,308). Do not cite until reconciled upstream. The underlying fact — that Chilean public finance was dominated by customs revenue through the saltpeter era — is documented in Mamalakis (1976) and remains historically true.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: Fiscal Revenue (millions)
curated_subtitle: Total fiscal revenue, 1810–1990, millions of pesos
definition: Total central-government fiscal revenue in millions of pesos. The nominal-peso unit crosses the 1925 (Central Bank founding), 1960 (escudo), and 1975 (new peso) currency reforms without rescaling at the cell level; the break-annotation register documents these. For deflated comparison, see fiscal_revenue_pesos at a narrower window or use the GDP-share series.

breaks.label: Decimal peso introduced
breaks.description: Chile decimalized its currency, replacing the colonial real-based system with a peso of 100 centavos. Pre-1851 fiscal series originate in reales and were converted to pesos at the source; conversions are not always documented per cell.
breaks.citation: Decreto sobre Monedas, 9 de enero de 1851; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Draw a thin vertical rule at 1851 on any nominal-peso series. Hover tooltip names the reform. Do not gray; the conversion is per-cell at the source.

breaks.label: Central Bank founded; peso pegged to gold
breaks.description: The Kemmerer mission of 1925 created the Banco Central de Chile (Decree-Law 486, August 1925) and pegged the peso to gold at 6 pence sterling. Pre-1925 fiscal and monetary series are nominal-peso, post-1925 reflect the gold-standard regime through its 1932 abandonment.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 486 de 1925, creación del Banco Central de Chile; Edwin W. Kemmerer (1925), Report on the Central Bank of Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Draw a vertical rule at 1925; tooltip names Central Bank founding and the gold peg.

breaks.label: Escudo replaces peso (1,000 : 1)
breaks.description: By Decree-Law 13.305 of 1959 the escudo replaced the peso at 1 escudo = 1,000 old pesos, in force January 1, 1960. Any nominal series that crosses 1960 without a 1,000× rescaling will show a phantom 1,000-fold drop interpreted as economic collapse.
breaks.citation: Ley 13.305 de 1959; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Refuse to plot a nominal-currency series across 1960 without deflation. If the platform has no deflated companion series, gray the post-1960 segment and surface a notice. Real-peso, constant-1996-peso, and GBP series are unaffected and render normally.

breaks.label: New peso replaces escudo (1,000 : 1)
breaks.description: By Decree-Law 1,123 of September 29, 1975 the current peso replaced the escudo at 1 peso = 1,000 escudos, in force September 29, 1975. Any nominal series that crosses 1975 without rescaling will show a second phantom 1,000-fold drop on top of the 1960 break.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 1,123 de 1975; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Same treatment as the 1960 break. Cumulative effect of 1960 + 1975 means a 1959 escudo value and a 1975 peso value are 1,000,000× apart in nominal terms.

breaks.label: Tarapacá annexed (Treaty of Ancón)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Ancón (October 20, 1883) transferred Tarapacá Province from Peru to Chile. National-level population, fiscal-revenue, and saltpeter series jump because the territorial base grew; department- and province-level series are unaffected outside Tarapacá itself.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Ancón, October 20, 1883; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1883 on national-aggregate series. For department/province maps, Tarapacá appears in the data starting 1884 (1883 was a transition year); the choropleth should show this as new geography rather than missing data.

breaks.label: Antofagasta annexed (Pacto de Tregua)
breaks.description: The Pacto de Tregua of April 4, 1884 transferred Antofagasta from Bolivia to Chilean administration; the formal cession came with the Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904. The 1884 pact is the working date for the data; Antofagasta enters Chilean statistical reporting from this year.
breaks.citation: Pacto de Tregua, 4 de abril de 1884; Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1884. The pre-1883 baseline excludes both Tarapacá and Antofagasta and is the relevant comparison year for the new territories.

breaks.label: Tacna returned to Peru (Treaty of Lima)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Lima of June 3, 1929 settled the Tacna-Arica question: Tacna returned to Peru, Arica confirmed Chilean. National-aggregate series experience a small reduction in 1929; the Tacna department disappears from Chilean reporting after this date.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Lima, June 3, 1929; St. John (1992), The Bolivia–Chile–Peru Dispute.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1929 on national series with subnational composition. Department-level series for Tacna terminate in 1929 and should not be drawn beyond that year.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

breaks.label: Social security privatized (AFP system)
breaks.description: Decreto-Ley 3,500 of 1980 replaced Chile's pay-as-you-go pension system with private capitalized accounts (AFP), in force from May 1981. Fiscal series for pensions, social spending, and the labor-force participation of older workers are not comparable across this break.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 3,500 de 1980; Mesa-Lago (1994), Changing Social Security in Latin America.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1981 on fiscal and labor series. Tooltip names the AFP reform.

breaks.label: Anuario primary → Braun et al. compilation
breaks.description: For long-run national series (GDP, fiscal aggregates, foreign trade, demographics), the pre-1929 segment is typically primary archival readings from the Anuario, Sinopsis, and Memoria series, while the post-1929 segment relies on Braun et al. (1998), Mamalakis (1980), and Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner (DLW) compilations. The compilers harmonized definitions, sometimes with judgment calls that differ from the primary readings.
breaks.citation: Braun et al. (1998); Mamalakis (1980); Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption attributes by year-segment per audit Finding 4.2. No vertical rule required unless a definitional revision crossed this date.

label: Fiscal Surplus (% of GDP)
curated_subtitle: Fiscal surplus as share of GDP, 1810–1990, percent
definition: Central-government fiscal surplus (revenue minus expenditure) as a percentage of GDP. The Chilean fiscal record includes substantial deficits during the wars of independence (1810–1818), the War of the Pacific (1879–1884), the 1930s Great Depression, and the 1971–1973 Unidad Popular years; the series captures all of these.

label: Fiscal balance (% of GDP)
definition: Central-government fiscal balance as a share of GDP, from Díaz, Lüders & Wagner (2016).

label: Fiscal surplus (real pesos)
definition: Central-government fiscal surplus expressed in real (deflated) pesos. The deflator and base year for the 'real pesos' construction are not standardized across the full 1810–1990 window; the convention shifts across publication periods, and the 1973–1976 inflation-index dispute documented in the break-annotation register affects the deflator for that window.

breaks.description: The Pacto de Tregua of April 4, 1884 transferred Antofagasta from Bolivia to Chilean administration; the formal cession came with the Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904. The 1884 pact is the working date for the data; Antofagasta enters Chilean statistical reporting from this year.
breaks.citation: Pacto de Tregua, 4 de abril de 1884; Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1884. The pre-1883 baseline excludes both Tarapacá and Antofagasta and is the relevant comparison year for the new territories.

breaks.label: Tacna returned to Peru (Treaty of Lima)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Lima of June 3, 1929 settled the Tacna-Arica question: Tacna returned to Peru, Arica confirmed Chilean. National-aggregate series experience a small reduction in 1929; the Tacna department disappears from Chilean reporting after this date.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Lima, June 3, 1929; St. John (1992), The Bolivia–Chile–Peru Dispute.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1929 on national series with subnational composition. Department-level series for Tacna terminate in 1929 and should not be drawn beyond that year.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

breaks.label: Social security privatized (AFP system)
breaks.description: Decreto-Ley 3,500 of 1980 replaced Chile's pay-as-you-go pension system with private capitalized accounts (AFP), in force from May 1981. Fiscal series for pensions, social spending, and the labor-force participation of older workers are not comparable across this break.
breaks.citation: Decreto-Ley 3,500 de 1980; Mesa-Lago (1994), Changing Social Security in Latin America.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1981 on fiscal and labor series. Tooltip names the AFP reform.

breaks.label: Anuario primary → Braun et al. compilation
breaks.description: For long-run national series (GDP, fiscal aggregates, foreign trade, demographics), the pre-1929 segment is typically primary archival readings from the Anuario, Sinopsis, and Memoria series, while the post-1929 segment relies on Braun et al. (1998), Mamalakis (1980), and Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner (DLW) compilations. The compilers harmonized definitions, sometimes with judgment calls that differ from the primary readings.
breaks.citation: Braun et al. (1998); Mamalakis (1980); Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption attributes by year-segment per audit Finding 4.2. No vertical rule required unless a definitional revision crossed this date.

breaks.label: INE inflation index dispute, 1973–1976
breaks.description: The Chilean inflation index published by INE during the 1973–1976 period was contested at the time and revised in later compilations. Real-peso deflators built from the official CPI for these years differ from later academic reconstructions (Cortázar and Marshall, 1980; Ffrench-Davis, 2001). Real-value series that span this window inherit the dispute.
breaks.citation: Cortázar and Marshall (1980), Indice de precios al consumidor en Chile, 1970–1978; Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Shaded background band over 1973–1976 on real-value series. Tooltip explains the deflator dispute. The platform should ideally offer the Cortázar-Marshall corrected series as a companion.

label: Government current revenue
definition: Current revenue of the general government, 1960-1970, from the national government accounts.
needs_user_decision: Memo Section 2: review whether this is 'current-year revenue' (merge into fiscal_revenue_millions) or 'budget current revenue' (rename for disambiguation).

breaks.label: Escudo replaces peso (1,000 : 1)
breaks.description: By Decree-Law 13.305 of 1959 the escudo replaced the peso at 1 escudo = 1,000 old pesos, in force January 1, 1960. Any nominal series that crosses 1960 without a 1,000× rescaling will show a phantom 1,000-fold drop interpreted as economic collapse.
breaks.citation: Ley 13.305 de 1959; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Refuse to plot a nominal-currency series across 1960 without deflation. If the platform has no deflated companion series, gray the post-1960 segment and surface a notice. Real-peso, constant-1996-peso, and GBP series are unaffected and render normally.

Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.

breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

label: Import capacity (YoY % change)
definition: Real value of exports adjusted by terms of trade, expressed as an index. The series measures the volume of imports Chile could buy with its export earnings, which captures terms-of-trade effects that simple export-volume series miss.

definition: Total imports as a percentage of GDP.

breaks.label: Tarapacá annexed (Treaty of Ancón)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Ancón (October 20, 1883) transferred Tarapacá Province from Peru to Chile. National-level population, fiscal-revenue, and saltpeter series jump because the territorial base grew; department- and province-level series are unaffected outside Tarapacá itself.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Ancón, October 20, 1883; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1883 on national-aggregate series. For department/province maps, Tarapacá appears in the data starting 1884 (1883 was a transition year); the choropleth should show this as new geography rather than missing data.

breaks.label: Dirección General de Estadística replaces OCE
breaks.description: The Oficina Central de Estadística (1843–1927) was reorganized into the Dirección General de Estadística in 1927. Several series carry definitional revisions across this transition (occupational classifications, education tabulations, foreign-trade reporting).
breaks.citation: Decreto sobre la Dirección General de Estadística, 1927; Hutchison (2001), Labors Appropriate to Their Sex.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption should attribute pre-1929 cells to OCE and post-1929 cells to DGE; no vertical rule required unless the user opens a definitional caveat.

curated_subtitle: Indirect taxes as share of fiscal revenue, 1833–1990, percent
definition: Revenue from indirect taxes (sales, VAT after 1975, excise, stamps) as a percentage of total fiscal revenue. Indirect taxation became dominant after the 1975 introduction of VAT (IVA); the series shows the transition.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: Intermediate goods imports
definition: Value of intermediate-goods imports (industrial inputs, semi-finished products). Series ends in 1937.
units_note: millions of US dollars

label: Internal public debt
curated_subtitle: Internal public debt outstanding, 1840–1897, millions of pesos
definition: Outstanding stock of domestic public debt in millions of pesos during the 19th century, source attribution incomplete in the manifest. The series ends in 1897; the post-1897 internal debt is captured under other indicators or remains to be added.

label: Internal taxes share of revenue
curated_subtitle: Internal taxes as share of fiscal revenue, 1833–1990, percent
definition: Revenue from internal taxes (all non-customs taxation) as a percentage of total fiscal revenue. The complement to external_taxes_share_of_revenue; together the two should sum to approximately 100 percent of tax revenue. Inherits the same unit-splice concerns.

label: Manufacturing GDP
definition: Real GDP of the manufacturing sector in constant 1996 pesos. The Chilean manufacturing sector grew under import-substitution industrialization (1930s–1973), contracted under the 1973–1983 liberalization, and stabilized at a lower share thereafter.

label: Mining GDP
definition: Real GDP of the mining sector in constant 1996 pesos. The series captures the nitrate era (peaking ca. 1929 at over 30 percent of GDP), the post-1929 collapse, the rise of copper, the 1971 nationalization, and the post-1990 expansion.

definition: Receipts of the national mint (Casa de Moneda).

label: Municipal central subsidy
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1875. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1875, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1886. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1885, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.

source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1875 (municipal finance)
definition: Annual subsidy from the central government to a department's municipal treasury, Sinopsis Estadística de Chile 1875–1895 municipal finance section. The subsidy was a substantial fraction of municipal budgets in poorer departments and a much smaller fraction in revenue-rich departments; the series documents the geography of central-government redistribution.
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'pesos' (1851 decimal peso, pre-1925 reform).

label: Municipal local revenue
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1907. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1907, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1875. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1875, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1895. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1895, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1907 (municipal finance)
definition: Municipal revenue raised locally (excluding central-government subsidy), department scale, Sinopsis Estadística de Chile 1875/1895/1907 municipal finance sections. Local revenue came from property taxes (contribuciones), license fees (patentes), and miscellaneous service fees; the composition varied by department.

label: Municipal police (ornato)
curated_subtitle: Municipal expenditure on policía de ornato, 1875–1895, pesos
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1895. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1895, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.

source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1895 (municipal finance)
definition: Municipal outlays on policía de ornato — the 19th-century Chilean term for municipal services covering public works, lighting, cleanliness, and building regulation — at department scale. From the Sinopsis Estadística municipal finance sections. The complement to muni_policia_seguridad (public order police).
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'pesos'.

label: Municipal police (seguridad)
curated_subtitle: Municipal expenditure on policía de seguridad, 1875–1895, pesos
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1895. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1895, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1875. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1875, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1895 (municipal finance)
definition: Municipal outlays on policía de seguridad — the 19th-century Chilean term for the municipal public-order police force — at department scale. From the Sinopsis Estadística municipal finance sections. The Chilean state developed a separate gendarmerie (Cuerpo de Gendarmes, later Carabineros from 1927) that gradually absorbed municipal seguridad functions; this series captures the pre-Carabineros municipal era.
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'pesos'.

label: Peso exchange rate

definition: Each province's share of national GDP, 1966–1970, source attribution incomplete in the manifest. Pre-CONARA-regionalization provinces; the 1974 regional reorganization (see break-annotation register) renders this geography historical.

label: Public education expenditure
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1861. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1860 (Tomo I). Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Anuario Estadístico 1860
definition: Total central-government expenditure on education, 1850-1858, summed across the funded institutions (Instituto Nacional, the university, the provincial liceos, the seminaries, the normal school and the arts academies).

label: Real GDP
definition: Chile's real gross domestic product expressed in constant 1996 pesos. The series is the canonical reconstruction of Chilean long-run output for the 1810–1990 period; pre-1860 figures are extrapolated from population and partial output data, and pre-1880 figures should be read as estimates rather than measurements.

breaks.label: Anuario primary → Braun et al. compilation
breaks.description: For long-run national series (GDP, fiscal aggregates, foreign trade, demographics), the pre-1929 segment is typically primary archival readings from the Anuario, Sinopsis, and Memoria series, while the post-1929 segment relies on Braun et al. (1998), Mamalakis (1980), and Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner (DLW) compilations. The compilers harmonized definitions, sometimes with judgment calls that differ from the primary readings.
breaks.citation: Braun et al. (1998); Mamalakis (1980); Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption attributes by year-segment per audit Finding 4.2. No vertical rule required unless a definitional revision crossed this date.

breaks.label: INE inflation index dispute, 1973–1976
breaks.description: The Chilean inflation index published by INE during the 1973–1976 period was contested at the time and revised in later compilations. Real-peso deflators built from the official CPI for these years differ from later academic reconstructions (Cortázar and Marshall, 1980; Ffrench-Davis, 2001). Real-value series that span this window inherit the dispute.
breaks.citation: Cortázar and Marshall (1980), Indice de precios al consumidor en Chile, 1970–1978; Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Shaded background band over 1973–1976 on real-value series. Tooltip explains the deflator dispute. The platform should ideally offer the Cortázar-Marshall corrected series as a companion.

label: Real GDP growth rate
definition: Year-over-year growth rate of real GDP. The 1810 cell carries a single-cell corruption (max value 139,277 percent vs the plausible series median around 3 percent) that the audit flags for upstream quarantine; the remaining series is interpretable.

breaks.label: Anuario primary → Braun et al. compilation
breaks.description: For long-run national series (GDP, fiscal aggregates, foreign trade, demographics), the pre-1929 segment is typically primary archival readings from the Anuario, Sinopsis, and Memoria series, while the post-1929 segment relies on Braun et al. (1998), Mamalakis (1980), and Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner (DLW) compilations. The compilers harmonized definitions, sometimes with judgment calls that differ from the primary readings.
breaks.citation: Braun et al. (1998); Mamalakis (1980); Díaz, Lüders, and Wagner.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption attributes by year-segment per audit Finding 4.2. No vertical rule required unless a definitional revision crossed this date.

breaks.label: INE inflation index dispute, 1973–1976
breaks.description: The Chilean inflation index published by INE during the 1973–1976 period was contested at the time and revised in later compilations. Real-peso deflators built from the official CPI for these years differ from later academic reconstructions (Cortázar and Marshall, 1980; Ffrench-Davis, 2001). Real-value series that span this window inherit the dispute.
breaks.citation: Cortázar and Marshall (1980), Indice de precios al consumidor en Chile, 1970–1978; Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Shaded background band over 1973–1976 on real-value series. Tooltip explains the deflator dispute. The platform should ideally offer the Cortázar-Marshall corrected series as a companion.

label: Real GDP per capita
curated_subtitle: Real GDP per capita, 1810–1990, constant 1996 pesos per capita
definition: Real GDP divided by total population. The most-cited indicator of Chilean long-run living standards. Chile's per-capita GDP fell behind Argentina's in the early 20th century, lost ground further during the 1970s, and converged again after 1985.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture

label: Real GDP per worker
curated_subtitle: Real GDP per worker, 1811–1990, constant 1996 pesos per worker
definition: Real GDP divided by total labor force. A productivity measure complementary to per-capita GDP; the divergence between the two reflects changes in the labor-force-to-population ratio (driven by demography, female participation, and age structure).

label: Revenue (% of GDP)
curated_subtitle: Fiscal revenue as share of GDP, 1810–1990, percent
definition: Total central-government fiscal revenue as a percentage of GDP. The published series carries severe unit-splice corruption: 180 of 181 non-null cells exceed 1,000 (against a natural ceiling of 100); the maximum observed value is 3,744,236 at 1972. Only the 1810 cell (3.76) is plausible. Do not cite this series until the upstream reconciliation lands.

label: Services GDP
definition: Real GDP of the services sector in constant 1996 pesos. The services sector includes commerce, transport, communications, finance, government, and personal services; the residual after the four goods-producing sectors.

label: Services share of GDP
curated_subtitle: Services as share of GDP, 1860–1990, percent
definition: Services sector value-added as a percentage of GDP. The services share rose from roughly 35 percent in 1860 to roughly 55 percent by 1990, tracking Chile's structural transformation from a primary-export to a services-anchored economy.

source_document: Véjares, compiled fiscal and customs series (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Total registered tonnage of vessels entering Chilean ports.

label: Subsidy share of revenue
curated_subtitle: Central subsidy share of municipal revenue, 1875–1895, percent
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1875. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1875, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1886. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1885, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1895. Sinopsis Estadística de la República de Chile, año 1895, municipal finance section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1875 (municipal finance)
definition: Central-government subsidy as a percentage of total municipal revenue at department scale, Sinopsis Estadística 1875–1895 municipal finance sections. Measures the dependence of municipal finance on central-government transfers; higher in frontier and resource-poor departments, lower in Santiago and Valparaíso.

label: Total exports
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1927. Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1924–1927. Santiago.
source_document: Dirección General de Estadística. 1927. Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1924–1927. Santiago.
definition: Total value of Chilean exports, 1862-1924, in pesos of 18 pence, from the foreign-trade series.

breaks.label: Tarapacá annexed (Treaty of Ancón)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Ancón (October 20, 1883) transferred Tarapacá Province from Peru to Chile. National-level population, fiscal-revenue, and saltpeter series jump because the territorial base grew; department- and province-level series are unaffected outside Tarapacá itself.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Ancón, October 20, 1883; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1883 on national-aggregate series. For department/province maps, Tarapacá appears in the data starting 1884 (1883 was a transition year); the choropleth should show this as new geography rather than missing data.

breaks.label: Antofagasta annexed (Pacto de Tregua)
breaks.description: The Pacto de Tregua of April 4, 1884 transferred Antofagasta from Bolivia to Chilean administration; the formal cession came with the Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904. The 1884 pact is the working date for the data; Antofagasta enters Chilean statistical reporting from this year.
breaks.citation: Pacto de Tregua, 4 de abril de 1884; Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1884. The pre-1883 baseline excludes both Tarapacá and Antofagasta and is the relevant comparison year for the new territories.

source_documents: Aldana, Abelardo. 1914. Resumen de la Hacienda Pública de Chile, 1833–1914. Santiago: Imprenta Universitaria.
source_document: Aldana, Abelardo. 1914. Resumen de la Hacienda Pública de Chile, 1833–1914. Santiago: Imprenta Universitaria.
definition: Total fiscal expenditure of the Chilean state, 1901-1913, in current pesos (moneda corriente).

display_label: Total fiscal revenue (British Foreign Office returns)

definition: Total Chilean fiscal revenue, 1840-1860, as compiled in the statistical returns of the British Foreign Office. An external check on the domestic fiscal accounts.

label: Total fiscal revenue (British Foreign Office, GBP)
display_label: Total fiscal revenue (British Foreign Office, GBP)
curated_subtitle: total fiscal revenue (British Foreign Office, GBP), national, 1833–1866, GBP

source_document: Aldana, Abelardo. 1914. Resumen de la Hacienda Pública de Chile, 1833–1914. Santiago: Imprenta Universitaria.
definition: Total Chilean fiscal revenue as reported, in pounds sterling, by the British Foreign Office statistical returns.

breaks.label: Decimal peso introduced
breaks.description: Chile decimalized its currency, replacing the colonial real-based system with a peso of 100 centavos. Pre-1851 fiscal series originate in reales and were converted to pesos at the source; conversions are not always documented per cell.
breaks.citation: Decreto sobre Monedas, 9 de enero de 1851; Banco Central de Chile, Historia Monetaria de Chile.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Draw a thin vertical rule at 1851 on any nominal-peso series. Hover tooltip names the reform. Do not gray; the conversion is per-cell at the source.

label: Boys' schools
curated_subtitle: Boys' schools at province scale, 1853–1858, count
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1861. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1860 (Tomo I), schools by province, 1853–1858. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
definition: Number of boys' schools by province in the 1850s, Anuario Estadístico 1860 series. The Chilean state's first systematic effort to enumerate primary education by sex and locality; coverage is partial and the unit string in the manifest is currently blank (it should read 'schools'). Per the duplicate-resolution memo, this indicator absorbs the parallel escuelas_hombres_prov entry.
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'schools'.

definition: Total enrollment in higher education institutions (universities, technical institutes). The Chilean higher-education system was small and elite through the 19th century and expanded sharply after 1960; the series tracks both phases.

label: Enfranchised per capita
curated_subtitle: Enfranchised voters per capita, by department, 1862–1920
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Landholding and Democracy in Chile. Working paper, Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1872. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, Tomo XI, años 1870–1871. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. ca. 1885. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, Tomo XXIV. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.

Oficina Central de Estadística. 1863. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1862, Entrega Quinta. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Véjares, *Landholding and Democracy in Chile*
definition: Number of enfranchised voters per inhabitant at department scale, Véjares Landholding and Democracy in Chile. The 19th-century Chilean franchise was restricted by literacy and property requirements that varied across the 1828, 1833, 1874, and 1888 electoral reforms; the per-capita series tracks how the effective franchise widened (or didn't) across departments.

label: Enfranchised voters (calificados/inscriptos)
curated_subtitle: Enfranchised voters at department scale, 1862–1921, persons
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Landholding and Democracy in Chile. Working paper, Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.

Oficina Central de Estadística. ca. 1885. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, Tomo XXIV. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1882. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1881, enfranchisement section. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Véjares, Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Number of enfranchised voters (calificados, the term used in the 19th-century electoral law) by department, Véjares department-level historical dataset. The base for the enfranchised_pc series and the analytical core of the Landholding paper's argument about landlord influence on the 19th-century franchise.

label: Registered voters (turnout panel)
curated_subtitle: Registered voters at department scale, 1888–1915, persons
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1892. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, Tomo XXVI, años 1887–1888. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
Dirección del Rejistro Electoral. 1915. Censo Electoral de la República de Chile, 1915. Santiago.
Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Landholding and Democracy in Chile. Working paper, Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Anuario Estadístico, Tomo XXVI, 1887–1888 (pub. 1892)
definition: Number of registered voters by department, Anuario Estadístico Tomo XXVI (1887–1888) and successor volumes. After the 1888 electoral reform, the registered-voter count became the operational measure of franchise extension; this series captures the post-1888 era. The pre-1888 calificados figure (enfranchised) is the conceptually closest predecessor.

label: Sufragantes (votes cast)
curated_subtitle: Voters who cast ballots at department scale, 1888–1915, persons
source_documents: Dirección del Rejistro Electoral. 1915. Censo Electoral de la República de Chile, 1915. Santiago.
Oficina Central de Estadística. 1892. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, Tomo XXVI, años 1887–1888. Santiago: Imprenta Nacional.
source_document: Censo Electoral de la República de Chile, 1915
definition: Number of voters who actually cast ballots in elections, by department, Anuario Estadístico Tomo XXVI and successor volumes. The ratio of sufragantes to register_voters is a measure of electoral participation; the gap between them captures abstention.

label: Votes cast in national elections
curated_subtitle: votes cast in national elections, national, 1935–1970, voters
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Compiled historical dataset, Chile, pre-1973, multi-source. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, compiled historical dataset (Chile, pre-1973, multi-source), author's compilation
definition: Votes cast in national elections, 1935-1970, from the electoral-participation series. Read against the registered and voting-age populations it tracks the mid-century expansion of the franchise.

label: Civil registry office present
curated_subtitle: Civil registry office present in department, 1885–1907
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, Maximiliano. Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation.
definition: Binary indicator: 1 if the department had a civil registry office (Oficina del Registro Civil) in the given year, 0 otherwise. Civil registration was created by the 1884 Ley de Registro Civil and rolled out over the following decade; the series captures the geographic diffusion of the modern Chilean state.

label: Departmental governor present
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. Multiple years. Presupuesto de la Nación, República de Chile. Santiago.
source_document: Presupuesto de la República de Chile
definition: Binary indicator: 1 if the department had a Gobernador (the central-government-appointed departmental executive under the 1833 constitution) listed in the national budget, 0 otherwise. From the Presupuesto de la República de Chile. Tracks the geographic reach of the central-government administrative apparatus.

label: Departmental judge present
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. Multiple years. Presupuesto de la Nación, República de Chile. Santiago.
source_document: Presupuesto de la República de Chile
definition: Binary indicator: 1 if the department had a Juez de Letras (the central-government-appointed departmental judicial officer) listed in the national budget, 0 otherwise. From the Presupuesto. Tracks the geographic reach of the Chilean judicial apparatus, which lagged the administrative apparatus in southern and frontier departments.

breaks.label: September 1973 — political rupture
breaks.description: The military government that took power on September 11, 1973 disrupted civilian statistical reporting for several years and introduced methodological changes in social and economic series. Fiscal, labor, and social-sector series across 1972–1976 are particularly affected by definitional turnover and, in some cases, political contestation of the published figures.
breaks.citation: Meller (2000); Ffrench-Davis (2001).
breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'tons'.

label: Private railway length
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1936. Anuario Estadístico de Chile, año 1935, Tabla 36. Santiago.
definition: Total kilometers of privately-owned railway track in Chile. The complement to state_owned_railway_length. The 19th-century railway buildout was a mix of private (mining and agricultural) and state-promoted lines; the 1884 nationalization of the central trunk reshaped the balance.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.
breaks.citation: Ley 17.374 de 1970 (15 de octubre de 1970), creación del Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Source caption transitions to INE for post-1970 cells. The 1976 labor-force redefinition is a separate event (see below).

label: Railway network length
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1936. Anuario Estadístico de Chile, año 1935, Tabla 36. Santiago.
source_document: Anuario Estadístico 1935, Tabla 36
definition: Total kilometers of railway track (private + state). The sum of private_railway_length and state_owned_railway_length; the indicator most directly useful for tracking the overall railway network's geographic extent.

label: State-owned railway length
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1936. Anuario Estadístico de Chile, año 1935, Tabla 36. Santiago.
definition: Total kilometers of state-owned railway track. The state railway operator (Empresa de los Ferrocarriles del Estado, EFE, founded 1884) became dominant in the central and southern Chilean network; the series shows its expansion through the 20th century and contraction after 1990.

label: Telephones in service
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1936. Anuario Estadístico de Chile, año 1935, Tabla 36. Santiago.
definition: Number of telephones in service in Chile. The series begins implausibly early (1868); the first commercial telephone service in Chile started in 1879. Pre-1879 figures should be read with skepticism or are likely encoding errors.

label: Minors under state protective control
curated_subtitle: minors under state protective control, national, 1821–1983, minors
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Compiled historical dataset, Chile, pre-1973, multi-source. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, compiled historical dataset (Chile, pre-1973, multi-source), author's compilation
definition: Minors held under state protective or correctional control, 1975-1983, from the juvenile-justice series.

label: Catastro avalúo (1874)
curated_subtitle: Total catastro appraised value, by department, 1874, pesos
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. 1875. Catastro de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874. Santiago.
source_document: Catastro de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874
definition: Total appraised value of taxable real property by department, from the Catastro de Avalúos de la República de Chile 1874. The denominator for the 1874 land-tax-base indicators; a single-year cross-section, not a time series.

label: Gini of avalúos (1874)
curated_subtitle: Gini coefficient of avalúos, by department, 1874
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. 1875. Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874, departmental returns. Santiago.
source_document: Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874 (departmental)
definition: Gini coefficient of property appraised values within each department, computed from the Rol de Avalúos 1874. A single-year cross-section. The indicator captures within-department inequality in landownership at one moment in 1874; values closer to 1 indicate higher concentration.

label: Manorial treatment (1874)
source_documents: Bauer, Arnold J. 1975. Chilean Rural Society from the Spanish Conquest to 1930. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
source_document: Bauer (1975), Chilean Rural Society
definition: Indicator of whether a department was characterized by the manorial latifundio system in 1874, after Bauer (1975), Chilean Rural Society. A single-year cross-section used as a treatment variable in the Landholding paper. The specific coding scheme should be documented in the source paper.

label: SNA estate mean price (1874)
curated_subtitle: Mean SNA estate sale price, by department, 1874, pesos
source_documents: Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura. 1874. Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, Volume VI. Santiago.
source_document: Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, 1874, Volume VI
definition: Mean sale price of estates listed in the Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, Volume VI (1874), by department. A single-year cross-section. The SNA listings provided market valuations distinct from the official catastro avalúos.

label: SNA-listed estates (1874)
curated_subtitle: Number of estates in SNA listings, by department, 1874
source_documents: Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura. 1874. Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, Volume VI. Santiago.
source_document: Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, 1874, Volume VI
definition: Count of estates listed for sale in the Boletín de la Sociedad Nacional de Agricultura, Volume VI (1874), by department. A single-year cross-section. Captures the level of land-market activity by region in 1874.
units_note: count of estates (raw count)

label: Taxpayers in Rol (1874)
curated_subtitle: Property taxpayers on the rol, by department, 1874, count
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. 1875. Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874, departmental returns. Santiago.
source_document: Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874 (departmental)
definition: Number of distinct taxpayers listed in the Rol de Avalúos 1874, by department. A single-year cross-section. The numerator for measures of landownership concentration (when divided by total department population or by adult male population).

label: Top 5 avalúo share (1874)
curated_subtitle: Top-5% share of total avalúos, by department, 1874, percent
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. 1875. Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874, departmental returns. Santiago.
source_document: Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874 (departmental)
definition: Share of total department appraised value held by the top 5 percent of property owners (by avalúo), Rol de Avalúos 1874. A single-year cross-section. A direct concentration measure complementing the Gini.

label: Total avalúo Rol (1874)
curated_subtitle: Total avalúos on the rol, by department, 1874, pesos
source_documents: Ministerio de Hacienda. 1875. Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874, departmental returns. Santiago.
source_document: Rol de Avalúos de la República de Chile, 1874 (departmental)
definition: Sum of avalúos listed in the Rol de Avalúos 1874, by department. A single-year cross-section. Conceptually similar to avaluo_total_1874 but drawn from the rol rather than the catastro; the two figures may differ where the rol omits or differently treats marginal taxpayers.

label: Coal production
definition: Annual national coal production in metric tons. Chilean coal came primarily from the Arauco and Concepción coalfields; output rose through the 19th-century industrial era, peaked mid-20th century, and declined as cheaper imported coal and natural gas displaced domestic production.

Series originating from INE after 1970 use modern definitions, including the 1976 labor-force survey redesign that breaks unemployment comparability with pre-1976 figures.

breaks.rule_for_chart: Light vertical rule at 1973 on long-run series. Tooltip notes the political-economic discontinuity without making a substantive judgment. Comparison across this break is methodologically legitimate but readers should know the regime change.

label: Copper production
definition: Annual national copper production in metric tons. Chile was a major world copper producer through the 19th century (the Chuquicamata-era expansion came after 1915 under US Anaconda and Kennecott ownership; the 1971 nationalization placed the major mines under Codelco).

breaks.label: Antofagasta annexed (Pacto de Tregua)
breaks.description: The Pacto de Tregua of April 4, 1884 transferred Antofagasta from Bolivia to Chilean administration; the formal cession came with the Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904. The 1884 pact is the working date for the data; Antofagasta enters Chilean statistical reporting from this year.
breaks.citation: Pacto de Tregua, 4 de abril de 1884; Treaty of Peace and Friendship of 1904; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1884. The pre-1883 baseline excludes both Tarapacá and Antofagasta and is the relevant comparison year for the new territories.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC
breaks.description: The Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE), created by Law 17.374 of 1970, replaced SNEC and remains Chile's statistical institute today. label: Copper production value
curated_subtitle: copper production value, national, 1918–1923, gold pesos (18d)
source_documents: Dirección General de Estadística. 1927. Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1924–1927. Santiago.
source_document: Sinopsis Estadística de Chile, 1924–1927
definition: Value of copper production, 1918-1923, in gold pesos of 18 pence.

label: Gold production
definition: Annual national gold production in kilograms. Chilean gold output was modest after the colonial era; the series captures occasional rushes (the 1850s Atacama deposits, the 1930s Andacollo expansion) and small-scale persistent production.

breaks.label: INE replaces SNEC

label: Saltpeter production
definition: Annual national saltpeter (sodium nitrate) production in metric tons. The saltpeter industry dominated Chilean exports and fiscal revenue from the 1880s through 1929; the post-1930 decline was rapid and the series ends in 1984 when commercial production effectively ceased.

breaks.label: Tarapacá annexed (Treaty of Ancón)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Ancón (October 20, 1883) transferred Tarapacá Province from Peru to Chile. National-level population, fiscal-revenue, and saltpeter series jump because the territorial base grew; department- and province-level series are unaffected outside Tarapacá itself.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Ancón, October 20, 1883; Sater (2007), Andean Tragedy.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1883 on national-aggregate series. For department/province maps, Tarapacá appears in the data starting 1884 (1883 was a transition year); the choropleth should show this as new geography rather than missing data.

breaks.label: Tacna returned to Peru (Treaty of Lima)
breaks.description: The Treaty of Lima of June 3, 1929 settled the Tacna-Arica question: Tacna returned to Peru, Arica confirmed Chilean. National-aggregate series experience a small reduction in 1929; the Tacna department disappears from Chilean reporting after this date.
breaks.citation: Treaty of Lima, June 3, 1929; St. John (1992), The Bolivia–Chile–Peru Dispute.
breaks.rule_for_chart: Vertical rule at 1929 on national series with subnational composition. Department-level series for Tacna terminate in 1929 and should not be drawn beyond that year.

label: Silver production
definition: Annual national silver production in kilograms. The major Chilean silver booms (Chañarcillo from 1832, Caracoles from 1870) shaped 19th-century mining geography.

label: Sulfur production
definition: Annual national sulfur production in metric tons. A small but persistent volcanic-arc industry; the series ends in 1975 when domestic production was displaced by petroleum-derived sulfur.

label: Agricultural workers (rural laborers)
curated_subtitle: Agricultural workers at department scale, 1862–1920, persons (scheduled merge)
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
Dirección General de Estadística. 1918. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1917, enfranchisement section. Santiago.
source_document: Véjares, Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Department-level count of agricultural workers (the 19th-century Chilean rural laborer occupations: peón, inquilino, gañán, jornalero), Véjares department-level historical dataset. The 'peasant' label is editorially loaded; per the duplicate-resolution memo, this indicator is scheduled to merge into a renamed agricultural_workers_department. The historiographical debate over what to call this class is documented in Bauer (1975) and Bengoa (2015).
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'persons' or 'workers'.

label: Engineers
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Department-level count of persons reporting 'ingeniero' (engineer) as their occupation in the 1865–1920 censuses, Véjares department-level historical dataset. A small but informative measure of technical professions and the geographic distribution of the modernizing middle class.
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'persons'.

label: Lawyers
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Department-level count of persons reporting 'abogado' (lawyer) as their occupation in the 1865–1920 censuses, Véjares department-level historical dataset. A measure of the legal-administrative profession; lawyers were highly concentrated in Santiago and Valparaíso.
units_note: Unit field currently blank; should read 'persons'.

label: Military Personnel
curated_subtitle: Military personnel at department scale, 1865–1920, persons
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century. Compiled from primary archival sources. Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
source_document: Véjares, Department-level historical dataset (Chile, 19th c.), author's compilation
definition: Department-level count of persons reporting military occupations (oficial, soldado, marino) in the 1865–1920 censuses, Véjares department-level historical dataset. Concentrated in garrison towns (Santiago, Valparaíso, Concepción, post-WoP northern departments).
units_note: persons

label: Police officers

label: Active legislators
source_documents: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile. Reseñas Biográficas Parlamentarias. Santiago: BCN. [database].
source_document: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional, Legislators Database
definition: Number of legislators (diputados plus senadores) representing the department, Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional database. The 19th-century Chilean Congress had a small number of legislators per electoral district; counts of one or two were typical.

label: Enfranchised population total
source_documents: Oficina Central de Estadística. 1921. Anuario Estadístico de la República de Chile, año 1920. Santiago.

label: Hacendado legislators
source_documents: Véjares, Maximiliano. Forthcoming. Department-level historical dataset, Chile, 19th century (legislator–landowner linkage). Net Zero Industrial Policy Lab, Johns Hopkins University.
definition: Number of legislators representing the department who were classified as hacendados (large landowners) by occupation, source attribution incomplete in the manifest. A direct measure of landlord representation in the 19th-century Congress and a core variable in the Landholding paper's argument.

label: Max terms, single legislator (1833–1891)
